title: America Was Built on Taking Risks. Today's Teens No Longer Do
gist: IFS Executive Director Carter Skeel argues that a multi-decade collapse in American teenagers' real-world risk-taking — drinking, trespassing, dating, socializing — mirrors and helps explain broader declines in marriage and childbearing, and calls for deliberately rebuilding a culture of small, personal risk-taking to sustain a flourishing society.

themes.theme: The data: teen risk-taking has collapsed

insights.claim: The share of 12th graders who have ever consumed alcohol fell from 92% in 1976 to 47% in 2024, according to the Monitoring the Future Study.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: A stark generational data point for any model of declining teen risk-taking and its downstream effects on young-adult life-milestone timing.
insights.quote: The number of 12th graders who have ever consumed alcohol dropped from 92% in 1976 to 47% in 2024.
insights.locator: To put an even finer point on it

insights.claim: The share of 12th-grade boys who report having trespassed fell from 31% to 15% over the same multi-decade period, per the Monitoring the Future Study.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Reinforces the pattern of declining rule-breaking and adventurous behavior among teens across multiple independent measures.
insights.quote: The number of 12th grade boys who say they have trespassed is down from 31% to just 15 percent.
insights.locator: To put an even finer point on it

insights.claim: Hours young adults report socializing with friends have more than halved over the past 20 years, from 12.3 hours to 5.1 hours, according to the American Time Use Survey.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Quantifies the collapse in in-person socializing among young adults — a leading indicator for dating-app dependence, loneliness-economy demand, and delayed relationship formation.
insights.quote: the number of hours young adults report socializing with friends has dropped by more than half in the last 20 years, from 12.3 hours to 5.1
insights.locator: Data from the American Time Use Survey

insights.claim: The General Social Survey finds that fewer young adults are having sex than in the past.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Another independent data source corroborating a broader retreat from interpersonal risk and intimacy among young people, relevant to fertility and relationship-formation forecasting.

insights.claim: In a landmark IFS survey of 24,000 parents conducted with Jonathan Haidt's Anxious Generation Project, most parents report not allowing their children to venture far from home even as teenagers.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Direct evidence linking parental risk-aversion to teens' reduced independence — a mechanism relevant to the 'anxious generation'/free-range-parenting policy debate.
insights.quote: most American kids aren’t even allowed by parents to venture far from home, even as teenagers
insights.locator: Our own landmark survey of 24,000 parents

insights.claim: Skeel argues American parenting now combines low tolerance for real-world risk-taking with high tolerance for unsupervised time in digital spaces, and that this imbalance can channel children's curiosity into unhealthy, anti-social online pursuits instead of real-world development.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Names a specific behavioral substitution (real-world risk to digital-world time) that is a leading indicator for the screen-time and social-media policy debate and related consumer-tech demand.
insights.quote: low parental tolerance for risk-taking in the real world, yet increasing amounts of time spent in unsupervised digital worlds
insights.locator: The IFS survey reflects a broader phenomenon

themes.theme: Downstream effects on marriage and fertility

insights.claim: Psychologist Jean Twenge's research shows teen risk aversion extends to long-term life choices: fewer high school boys now expect to attend college, and fewer high school boys and girls expect to get married than in the past.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Links declining teen risk tolerance directly to reduced marriage and education expectations — a mechanism for forecasting continued delay in household formation.
insights.locator: As psychologist Jean Twenge has convincingly shown

insights.claim: IFS demographer Wendy Wang's research finds falling U.S. fertility is primarily driven by fewer women having children in their 20s; Skeel argues that if today's teen risk-aversion trends persist, delays in marriage and childbearing will continue into the next generation.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Directly ties teen-behavior data to the broader fertility-decline story, projecting continued downward pressure on household formation.
insights.quote: If the trends among teenagers are any indication, delays in marriage and child-rearing will only continue as young adults enter adulthood lacking in major life experiences.
insights.locator: Already, IFS demographer Wendy Wang finds

insights.claim: Skeel's central thesis is that risk aversion is not a foundation for a flourishing or self-sustaining society, arguing that America's major historical achievements — the Revolution, the moon landing, westward expansion — all required sustained risk-taking.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Frames the teen-risk-aversion data as a civilizational-level warning sign rather than just a youth-culture curiosity — relevant to any long-horizon thesis on U.S. dynamism and entrepreneurship rates.
insights.quote: Risk aversion is not the foundation for a flourishing, or even self-sustaining, society.
insights.locator: Risk aversion is not the foundation

insights.claim: Skeel cites NASA's Artemis II mission — the first crewed U.S. lunar flyby in roughly 50 years, conducted earlier in 2026 — as evidence that America retains the capacity for bold, large-scale risk-taking projects.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: A concrete, timely example (2026 Artemis II) that could be cited to counter narratives of full-scale American risk-aversion — relevant to space and aerospace sentiment and government big-project funding debates.
insights.quote: The Artemis II mission conducted earlier this year, NASA’s first lunar flyby in a half-century, is a remarkable testament to our continuous capacity for bold projects.
insights.locator: Some of these risks may be large projects

insights.claim: Skeel's prescription for renewal centers on small, personal risks — asking someone on a date, getting involved at church, accepting a marriage proposal, starting a company, having kids, moving to a new town — rather than only large civilizational projects.
insights.speaker: Carter Skeel
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete behavioral checklist that pronatalist and family-formation advocacy groups could use as messaging, and signals what 'success' looks like from IFS's institutional perspective.
insights.quote: asking a girl out on a date, getting involved at church, saying yes to a marriage proposal, starting a new company, having kids, moving to a new town
insights.locator: But most of these risks will be small
